lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: This is the indictment of the good Lord Hastings;

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: Which in a set hand fairly is engross’d,

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: That it may be this day read over in Paul’s.

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: And mark how well the sequel hangs together:

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: Eleven hours I spent to write it over,

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: For yesternight by Catesby was it brought me;

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: The precedent was full as long a-doing:

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: And yet within these five hours lived Lord Hastings,

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: Here’s a good world the while! Why who’s so gross,

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: That seeth not this palpable device?

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: Yet who’s so blind, but says he sees it not?

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: Bad is the world; and all will come to nought,

lines.speaker: SCRIVENER
lines.text: When such bad dealings must be seen in thought.

stage_directions.text: Enter a Scrivener, with a paper in his hand.

glosses.definition: in a formal hand carefully inscribed — legal scribal vocabulary
read aloud at St Paul's Cross — the public site for royal proclamations
the rough draft from which the fair copy was made
obvious trick — the scrivener names the fraud: the indictment was prepared before Hastings was arrested

annotations.title: The scrivener as one-scene truth-teller
annotations.body: The scrivener's fourteen-line scene is the shortest in the play and one of its most precisely calibrated. A legal scribe, alone, explains that the indictment against Hastings was drawn up before Hastings was accused, that the precedent (draft) took as long as the fair copy, and that the whole operation is a "palpable device." The word "palpable" is key — the fraud is not subtle, it is obvious to anyone who handles the paper. The scrivener's closing couplet — "Bad is the world; and all will come to nought, / When such bad dealings must be seen in thought" — is a choric judgement from a minor character whose profession gives him uniquely reliable access to the regime's documentary practice. Shakespeare keeps moral clarity alive in the play by distributing it across scenes and minor voices. The scrivener will not appear again; his sentence is the whole scene.

trials.prompt: What does the scrivener reveal about the indictment against Hastings?
options: That it was drafted before Hastings was ever accused — it is a "palpable device" visible to anyone handling the document
That Hastings himself had signed it in advance
That the charges are limited to minor offences

trials.prompt: The scrivener's fourteen-line scene functions as what?
options: Accidental material, carried over from an earlier draft
A choric judgement from a minor professional voice — moral clarity distributed to a figure with uniquely reliable access to the regime's paperwork
A piece of comic relief without dramatic purpose
A digression on Elizabethan legal practice
